---
title: "Executive Summary: FOMC Event-Window Strategy"
subtitle: "SPY Contrarian Fade at 2:00 PM ET | MGMTMFE 412 — Market Frictions"
author: "UCLA Anderson School of Management — Spring 2025"
date: today
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    geometry: "margin=1.1in, top=0.9in, bottom=0.9in"
    fontsize: 11pt
    number-sections: false
    colorlinks: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{booktabs}
        \usepackage{float}
---

## Intuition

Eight times a year, at exactly 2:00 PM ET, the Federal Reserve announces its interest
rate decision. What happens in the next 60 seconds is remarkably consistent:
algorithmic trading systems parse the statement in microseconds and flood the market
with buy or sell orders — all in the same direction, all at the same time.

The problem is that the order book is nearly empty at that moment. In the 30 minutes
before the announcement, market makers — anticipating that they will be on the wrong
side of informed traders — have already widened their spreads and pulled their limit
orders. When the HFT wave hits a thin book, prices move **more than they should**.

This overshoot is not permanent. Over the following 10–20 minutes, as human analysts
read the full statement, press conference, and dot plot, the market corrects back
toward fair value. **The strategy bets on that correction.**

---

## The Friction That Creates the Opportunity

The pre-announcement spread widening is the key mechanism. It is not incidental — it
is the *direct* consequence of adverse-selection theory: market makers price in the
risk of trading against better-informed participants.

Measured across all five years in our sample, the spread in the 30 minutes before
the FOMC announcement is systematically 10–30% wider than during the rest of the day.
On matched non-FOMC control days, the same window shows no widening at all.

This widened spread does two things simultaneously: it **amplifies the overshoot**
(by thinning the book) and it **raises the cost of entry** (by making the 2:01 PM
trade more expensive). The friction creates the opportunity while also limiting it.

---

## Strategy

At **2:01 PM** — one minute after the announcement — we observe the direction of the
2:00 PM bar and trade against it:

- If SPY rallied in the 2:00 PM bar → **short** SPY, exit at 2:15 PM
- If SPY fell in the 2:00 PM bar → **long** SPY, exit at 2:15 PM

No forecasting of the Fed decision is needed. We are not predicting policy — we are
betting that whatever the initial algorithmic reaction was, it went too far.

---

## Results

Tested across **40 FOMC events from 2021 to 2025**, the strategy's performance
depends sharply on the policy regime:

**It works when the decision is anticipated** (hold, or cut as expected). In these
32 events, the initial move was mechanical noise on top of small fundamental news,
and it reversed. The strategy generated +\$2,354 net PnL with a Sharpe ratio of 0.83
and a p-value of 0.048 — statistically significant.

**It breaks when the Fed is in an aggressive hiking cycle** (2022). In those 8 events,
the initial drop was not overshoot — it was the market repricing the entire yield
curve. The strategy lost −\$787, going long into a market that kept falling for hours.

The **placebo test** confirms the edge is FOMC-specific: running the identical strategy
on 39 matched non-FOMC control days produces essentially zero net PnL across five years.

| Scenario | Events | Net PnL | Sharpe | p-value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hold / cut cycles (2021, 2023–2025) | 32 | +\$2,354 | 0.83 | 0.048 |
| Aggressive hiking cycle (2022) | 8 | −\$787 | −0.53 | 0.61 |
| Non-FOMC control days (placebo) | 39 | +\$278 | +0.15 | 0.75 |

---

## The Key Risk

The December 18, 2024 meeting illustrates the primary failure mode. The Fed cut rates
as expected — but then delivered unexpectedly hawkish guidance about fewer cuts in
2025. SPY fell. The strategy went long. SPY kept falling for the rest of the day.

When the Fed delivers a surprise in *guidance* rather than (or in addition to) the
rate decision itself, the initial move is not overshoot — it is new information being
processed. This type of "dual surprise" cannot be filtered in advance, and a single
event can erase several months of gains.

---

## Connection to Market Frictions

This strategy is the mirror image of the lead-lag study. There, the bid-ask spread
*prevented* a real alpha from being exploited. Here, the same spread mechanism —
driven by the same adverse-selection logic — *creates* the trading opportunity by
thinning the book and amplifying the HFT overshoot.

> **Friction is not uniformly harmful.** Whether it kills an opportunity or creates
> one depends entirely on which side of the information event you are positioned on.
